author.name: Bradley A. Pistotnik (Trial Lawyer)
author.bio: Brad Pistotnik is the CEO of Bull Attorneys®, P.A. and a Kansas trucking and personal injury lawyer with over 45 years of experience. He focuses on serious injury and truck accident cases involving DOT regulations and commercial vehicle safety standards.

sections.content: No family is ever ready for a fatal car accident. A wrongful death claim cannot undo the loss — but it may help your family seek accountability and financial support during the hardest time of your lives.

left.heading: What Is a Wrongful Death Claim?
paragraphsHtml: Kansas law says that if the death of a person is caused by the wrongful act or omission of another, a claim may be brought for the damages that result from that death.
A wrongful death case is not a criminal case. It is a civil claim for financial recovery and accountability — separate from any criminal matter.

right.heading: When a Fatal Crash May Lead to a Claim
right.intro: A wrongful death case may follow when negligence caused the death.
items: A wreck caused by a drunk or distracted driver
A semi-truck or commercial vehicle accident

leftCard.heading: Who May Have Rights in a Kansas Wrongful Death Case?
leftCard.intro: In Kansas, people who are heirs-at-law may have the right to recover. Examples include:
leftCard.footnote: Because family situations can be complex, it is important to get clear legal guidance early.

rightCard.heading: What Damages May Be Available?
items: Pain and suffering where allowed by law
Other damages supported by the facts

sections.label: TIME MATTERS

image.alt: Damaged vehicle at a crash scene at night

sections.heading: Why Fast Action Matters in a Fatal Accident Case
sections.intro: Important evidence can disappear if action is delayed. A fast investigation may help protect the facts and reduce the chance that key proof is lost.

sections.label: KANSAS TRAFFIC FATALITIES SNAPSHOT
sections.heading: Kansas Road Fatalities Show the Risk Is Real
sections.intro: KDOT's Drive To Zero page shows progress — but families across Kansas are still losing loved ones in traffic accidents.

primaryStats.label: Truck cases may involve company records & commercial insurance
Wrongful death is a civil claim, separate from criminal charges

faqs.question: What is a wrongful death claim in Kansas?
faqs.answer: A wrongful death claim is a civil case that may be brought when a person dies because of the wrongful act or omission of another person or business.

faqs.question: Who may have rights in a Kansas wrongful death case?
faqs.answer: Bull Attorneys' wrongful death page explains that heirs-at-law may have the right to recover, including close family members such as a surviving spouse, children, grandparents, or siblings.

faqs.question: What damages may be available in a wrongful death car accident case?
faqs.answer: Depending on the facts, damages may include funeral expenses, medical bills from before death, lost financial support, loss of services, and other damages allowed by law.

faqs.question: Why is fast action important after a fatal car accident?
faqs.answer: Important evidence may include accident reports, witness statements, vehicle data, and business records. In truck or company-vehicle cases, additional records may also need to be preserved quickly.

faqs.question: Is a wrongful death claim the same as a criminal case?
faqs.answer: No. A wrongful death claim is a civil case for financial recovery and accountability. It is separate from any criminal matter.

schema.description: A wrongful death claim cannot undo the loss — but it may help your family seek accountability and financial support. Learn how Kansas wrongful death law works.
keywords: wrongful death car accident lawyer Kansas
schema.articleBody: In Kansas, a wrongful death claim may be filed when the death of a person is caused by the wrongful act or omission of another. It is a civil claim — not a criminal case — for financial recovery and accountability. Fatal crashes involving drunk or distracted drivers, commercial vehicles, semi-trucks, or high-speed collisions may lead to wrongful death claims. Heirs-at-law including surviving spouses, children, grandparents, and siblings may have the right to recover. Damages may include funeral expenses, medical bills from before death, lost financial support, loss of companionship, and other damages supported by the facts. Fast action matters — evidence like accident reports, witness statements, vehicle data, and company records can disappear if action is delayed. Kansas reported 136 traffic fatalities as of June 16, 2026, a 24% decrease from the same date in 2025, but families across Kansas are still losing loved ones in traffic accidents.

disclaimer: This blog provides general information only, not legal advice. Reading it does not create an attorney-client relationship. Every case is different. Results vary depending on each case's facts and legal circumstances. Deadlines and laws can change, so speak directly with an attorney about your accident.
